title: decode_interleaved returns Option<(Vec<S>,u32,u32)> but internal run_raw returns (w,h,meta,data) — callers silently discard meta, hiding a contract difference from the object API
description: The free-fn `decode_interleaved` (and its wrappers `decode_jxl_rgba8`, `decode_jxl_rgba16`) return `Option<(Vec<S>, u32, u32)>` while every object-API method on `Decoder` returns `Result<Image<S>, DecodeError>` which contains `meta`, `channels`, and `extra`. Callers of the free-fns cannot observe `DecodedMeta` (e.g. `has_alpha`, `bits_per_sample`, `num_extra_channels`). This means the two public API surfaces promise different richness for the same decode operation — a contract drift that silently drops metadata that consumers may need to validate pixel formats. The declared return type is not technically wrong, but it is a narrowing contract relative to the object API with no documentation of the omission.

title: decode_jxl_rgba16 reinterprets u16 pixels as native-endian bytes but its declared return type Vec<u8> is undocumented as 'packed NE u16 bytes', not real RGBA8
description: `decode_jxl_rgba16` returns `Option<(Vec<u8>, u32, u32)>` — the same shape as `decode_jxl_rgba8` — but the bytes inside are native-endian packed `u16` samples (8 bytes/pixel), not 4-byte RGBA8. A caller treating the output as RGBA8 (e.g. feeding it to `ImageData` or a shader expecting 4 bytes/pixel) will silently misinterpret the layout. The return type `Vec<u8>` gives no signal that the content is `u16 NE` bytes; the docstring says 'matching the prior jpegxl-sys path' but makes no mention of the consumer responsibility to reinterpret as `u16`.

title: Decoder::decode and decode_into accept channels=0 via Channels enum but internal pixel-format math will call libjxl with num_channels=0, likely crashing or returning garbage
description: The public `Channels` enum only exposes `Gray`/`GrayAlpha`/`Rgb`/`Rgba` (counts 1–4) so this is not reachable from typed callers. However, `run_raw` (called by `decode_interleaved`) accepts an arbitrary `channels: u32` argument with no validation before passing it to `pixel_format::<S>(channels)` which feeds it directly to `JxlPixelFormat.num_channels`. A caller passing `channels=0` (possible since `run_raw` is `pub(crate)` and accessible within the same module) would pass 0 to libjxl, which is undefined behaviour. The boundary between the typed public API and the raw internal `channels: u32` path has no guard.

title: Decoder::set_options silently replaces opts.parallel without rebuilding the runner, leaving runner pointer and opts.parallel out of sync
description: `set_options` replaces `self.opts` wholesale. If a caller constructs `Decoder::new(DecodeOptions { parallel: false, .. })` (runner=null) and then calls `set_options(DecodeOptions { parallel: true, .. })`, the new opts say parallel but `self.runner` is still null — the parallel runner is never attached and encoding silently runs single-threaded. The inverse (parallel=true → false) leaves a non-null runner that will still be attached by `attach_runner`. The contract 'opts.parallel == (runner is non-null)' is an invariant the object asserts at construction but `set_options` cannot maintain it.

title: Encoder::set_options has the same runner/opts.parallel invariant break as the decoder
description: `Encoder::set_options` replaces the full `EncodeOptions` struct without touching `self.runner`. If a caller created an `Encoder::with_threads` (runner non-null) and calls `set_options` with a brand-new `EncodeOptions` that has a different implied threading intent, the runner still gets re-attached on the next `encode_inner`. More critically there is no way to disable or replace the runner via `set_options` since `runner` is private and not part of `EncodeOptions`. This is a one-way ratchet: once a threaded encoder is created, threading cannot be turned off via the public API.

title: JXTC_HEADER_BYTES=32 and JXTC_INDEX_ENTRY_BYTES=8 are public consts but there is no static assertion or test that the parse/write layout actually matches these constants
description: `parse_jxtc_header` slices `data[0..4]`, `data[4..8]`, ..., `data[28..32]` for exactly 8 u32 fields — the layout is implicitly 8×4 = 32 bytes, matching `JXTC_HEADER_BYTES`. `JXTC_INDEX_ENTRY_BYTES=8` (two u32s per entry) is similarly implicit. If a future writer adds a 9th field before `flags` (shifting `flags` to offset 32), `parse_jxtc_header` silently reads the wrong bytes for `flags` without any compile-time or runtime mismatch. There is no writer-side test that encodes a JXTC container and round-trips through `parse_jxtc_header`; the tests only exercise the tile-decode path, not the header format contract.

title: EncodeOptions.effort field is declared u8 but libjxl's valid range is 1..=10; no validation guard exists — effort=0 or effort>10 silently passes to JxlEncoderFrameSettingsSetOption
description: `EncodeOptions.effort: u8` accepts 0..=255. The default is 3 (valid). `with_effort` sets the field directly without bounds checking. `encode_inner` calls `set_opt(fs, FrameSettingId::Effort, self.opts.effort as i64)` which calls `JxlEncoderFrameSettingsSetOption` with whatever value was stored. libjxl clamps effort internally on some builds but the public contract of `EncodeOptions.effort` does not document this range, and callers using `.with_effort(0)` or `.with_effort(200)` receive no error at the Rust API boundary.

title: Rate::Distance(f32) has no range guard — negative or NaN distance values silently reach JxlEncoderSetFrameDistance
description: `Rate::Distance(d)` accepts any `f32` including negative, NaN, or infinity. `encode_inner` directly calls `ffi::JxlEncoderSetFrameDistance(fs, d)` with the unchecked value. libjxl may treat negative distance as undefined behaviour or return a non-success status (caught by `check()`), but a NaN value will cause `check()` to see a libjxl error with no descriptive message beyond 'JxlEncoderSetFrameDistance failed'. The public `EncodeOptions::distance` constructor does not validate, and the `Distance` variant documentation says 'Butteraugli distance, 0..15' without enforcing it.

category: enum not handled by all consumers
title: DecodeEvent::Final carries Vec<S> (owned) but DecodeEvent::Progress carries &'a [S] (borrowed) — the asymmetry is undocumented and forces callers to pattern-match differently for what is semantically the 'same' pixel update
description: `DecodeEvent::Progress` lends `pixels: &'a [S]` while `DecodeEvent::Final` gives `pixels: Vec<S>`. This is a deliberate optimization (avoid a clone for the final frame) but it breaks the 'uniform event' expectation: a caller accumulating frames cannot write a single arm that works for both variants. The asymmetry is not documented in the enum or the `decode_progressive` doc comment. In `run_progressive_into` the `Final` variant is never emitted — it is only in the public enum shape, meaning callers who write a `Final` match arm expecting pixels from the callback will never see it (the return value `Image<S>` is the final frame).

title: jxl-ffi has only a smoke-test (version > 0); no contract test verifies that the allowlist in build.rs covers every symbol used by jxl_encode.rs and jxl_casadecoder.rs
description: The only test in `jxl-ffi` calls `JxlDecoderVersion()` to confirm the link works. If `build.rs`'s `allowlist_function/type/var` regexes are narrowed (e.g. a future build.rs edit removes `JxlThread.*` from the allowlist), the bindgen output silently drops `JxlThreadParallelRunner` and `JxlThreadParallelRunnerCreate`/`Destroy`, causing a downstream compile error in `jxl_encode.rs` and `jxl_casadecoder.rs` that is hard to attribute to the FFI layer. A contract test that calls at least one encode-path symbol and one thread-runner symbol would catch this at the `jxl-ffi` layer rather than in the dependent crate.

title: build.rs has no mechanism to detect or fail on an unexpected libjxl version mismatch — bindings regenerate silently against whatever CMakeLists.txt provides
description: The build script calls `JxlDecoderVersion()` nowhere at build time. If `external/libjxl` is updated to 0.12+ while callers in `jxl_encode.rs`/`jxl_casadecoder.rs` rely on 0.11 API shapes (e.g. field offsets in `JxlBasicInfo`), the bindgen output will change silently and the Rust code will compile against the new ABI while the test corpus still contains 0.11-encoded files. A `cargo:warning` or `assert!` on `JxlDecoderVersion()` >= some expected constant (emitted from a tiny probe build) would make version skew a hard build failure rather than a silent runtime mismatch.

title: decode_progressive_frames_borrowed emits Final only on success but never emits it on allow_partial truncated-input path, breaking the 'always get Final' contract callers expect
description: The legacy free-function `decode_progressive_frames_borrowed` emits `DecodeProgressiveEvent::Final` only after the loop exits with `S_FULL | S_SUCCESS` and the buffer is non-empty. When `S_NEEDIN` is hit (truncated input), the loop breaks without emitting `Final`, and the function returns `None`. However the caller-visible contract (`on_frame` receives `Progress*` events then exactly one `Final`) is broken silently — a caller holding partial frames never learns the stream ended early, it just sees `None` returned from the function. The object-API path (`Decoder::decode_progressive`) has the same gap via `run_progressive_into` — `allow_partial` breaks out of the loop but then returns a full `Image<S>` with no indication that the data is truncated.
